name: [TEST FIXTURE] Weather & Climate

guides.title: Reading weather radar
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Radar color scale typically runs green (light rain) → yellow (moderate) → red (heavy) → purple/pink (intense, often hail).

A hook echo (the radar image curling back on itself) often indicates rotation and a possible tornado. A bow echo (a line bending forward) usually marks a strong straight-line wind event. Both are warnings to take shelter, not to keep watching.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Lightning safety — the 30/30 rule
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

If you hear thunder within 30 seconds of seeing lightning, the storm is within ~6 miles — close enough to be struck. Get inside immediately.

Wait 30 minutes after the last thunder before going back out. Indoors, stay off corded phones, away from water, and away from windows. Outdoors with no shelter: avoid tall isolated objects, water, and metal. Crouch low with feet together, don't lie flat — keep contact with the ground minimal.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Heat illness — exhaustion vs stroke
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Heat exhaustion: heavy sweating, cool clammy skin, weak pulse, nausea, headache. Move to a cool place, loosen clothing, sip water, apply cool wet cloths. Recovery is usually fast.

Heat stroke is a life-threatening escalation: body temp 103°F+, hot dry red skin, rapid strong pulse, confusion, possible loss of consciousness. Call emergency services immediately. Cool the person aggressively — cold packs to the neck, groin, armpits; cool bath if possible. Do not give fluids if confused or unconscious.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Cold illness — hypothermia and frostbite
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Frostbite affects exposed skin: numbness, white or grayish appearance, hard or waxy texture. Get indoors. Warm the area with body heat or warm water (not hot, not direct flame). Do not rub.

For hypothermia symptoms see the shelter pack's hypothermia guide. Layered clothing, dry feet, and avoiding alcohol are the main prevention. Wind chill makes cold dangerous fast even when temps look manageable.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring under OFF-1373..1378 will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Watch vs warning
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

WATCH means conditions are favorable for severe weather to develop in the watch area within a few hours. Stay alert, review your plan, monitor updates.

WARNING means severe weather is occurring or imminent in the warned area. Take protective action immediately — shelter, evacuate, or whatever the specific warning calls for. Warnings are issued by your country's official weather service and broadcast on weather radios.
